title: Fix the Login Query
description: The login function below is vulnerable to SQL injection. Rewrite it using a parameterised query to make it secure.
instructions: Replace the string concatenation with a parameterised query. Use ? as the placeholder and pass values as a tuple to cursor.execute().
vulnerable_code:
def login(username, password):
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
    cursor.execute(query)
    return cursor.fetchone() is not None
hints: Use ? placeholders instead of string concatenation
Pass user values as a second argument tuple: cursor.execute(query, (username, password))
The query string itself should contain no variables — only ? placeholders
explanation: The original code concatenates username and password directly into the SQL string. An attacker can enter ' OR '1'='1 to bypass authentication. The secure version uses parameterised queries where the database driver handles escaping automatically — injection is structurally impossible.

title: Sanitise the Comment Display
description: This Flask route renders a user comment directly into HTML without escaping, making it vulnerable to XSS. Fix it using proper output escaping.
instructions: Import escape from markupsafe and apply it to the comment variable before embedding it in the HTML response.
hints: Add: from markupsafe import escape at the top
Apply escape() to the comment variable before embedding it
escape() converts < > & " to safe HTML entities
explanation: Without escaping, an attacker can inject <script>alert(1)</script> as a comment and the browser executes it. escape() converts dangerous characters to HTML entities so they display as visible text rather than executing as code.

title: Upgrade the Password Storage
description: This registration function stores passwords using MD5 — a broken hashing algorithm. Rewrite it to use bcrypt with a cost factor of 12.
instructions: Replace the MD5 hash with bcrypt. Import bcrypt, generate a salt with bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12), and hash with bcrypt.hashpw().
vulnerable_code:
import hashlib

def register_user(username, password):
    password_hash = hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()
    db.execute(
        'INSERT INTO users (username, password_hash) VALUES (?, ?)',
        (username, password_hash)
    )
hints: Import bcrypt at the top of your file
explanation: MD5 is fast — GPUs compute billions of MD5 hashes per second, making brute force trivial. bcrypt is intentionally slow with a configurable cost factor and automatically salts each hash to defeat rainbow table attacks.

title: Secure the File Download
description: This file download endpoint passes user input directly to the file system without validation, enabling path traversal attacks. Fix it to safely restrict access to the uploads directory.
instructions: Use os.path.realpath() to resolve the full path, then verify it starts with the intended base directory before serving the file. Return abort(403) if the path escapes.
hints: Use os.path.realpath() to resolve all ../ sequences in the path
Check: if not filepath.startswith(UPLOAD_DIR + os.sep): abort(403)
Also validate UPLOAD_DIR itself with realpath() at the top
explanation: os.path.join does not prevent traversal — ?file=../../../../etc/passwd produces a valid path to /etc/passwd. os.path.realpath() resolves all ../ sequences to absolute form. The startswith check then ensures the file must be inside the intended directory.

title: Replace the Unsafe Input Function
description: This C function uses gets() to read user input into a fixed-size buffer — a classic buffer overflow vulnerability. Replace it with fgets() and add proper null termination.
instructions: Replace gets(buffer) with fgets(buffer, sizeof(buffer), stdin). Then remove the trailing newline that fgets may include using strcspn.
hints: Replace gets(buffer) with fgets(buffer, sizeof(buffer), stdin)
fgets includes the newline — remove it with: buffer[strcspn(buffer, "\n")] = '\0'
sizeof(buffer) ensures the limit always matches the actual allocation
explanation: gets() reads with no length limit — input longer than 64 bytes overwrites adjacent stack memory and can lead to arbitrary code execution. fgets() limits the read to sizeof(buffer)-1 characters. The strcspn call removes the trailing newline that fgets includes.
